what: what does ... do in an object
why: why use spread instead of object.assign
why did spread overwrite my property
how: how to merge objects with spread
output: spread operator creates a new object

1_beginner_explanation.summary: The **Spread Operator** (`...`) allows you to take an existing object and 'spread' (unpack) all of its key-value pairs into a brand new object. It is the modern, cleanest way to copy an object or merge two objects together. Just like `Object.assign`, if there are duplicate keys, the ones listed last (furthest down) will overwrite the earlier ones.
1_beginner_explanation.analogy: Imagine you have a customized pizza (the old object). Using the spread operator is like telling the chef: 'Make me a brand new pizza, put all the exact same toppings from that first pizza on it (`...oldPizza`), and then also add extra pepperoni on top'.

2_internal_mechanism.execution_flow: When the engine evaluates `{ ...obj1, ...obj2 }`, it allocates memory for a new object literal. It iterates over `obj1`'s enumerable properties, defining them on the new object. Then it does the same for `obj2`. It performs a shallow copy.
2_internal_mechanism.memory: Unlike `Object.assign(obj1, obj2)` which mutates `obj1`, the spread syntax strictly defines properties on a newly allocated object literal.

3_real_world_example.use_case: Updating a user's status in a Redux/React application without mutating the original state object.

4_common_confusion.trap: Putting the spread operator *after* your new properties. If you write `{ isOnline: true, ...userState }`, the `userState` gets unpacked last, and its `isOnline: false` will overwrite your `true`!
4_common_confusion.fix: Order matters. Always spread the base object first, and put your overrides/updates at the bottom.

6_edge_case.quirk: The same `...` syntax is used for the Rest pattern during destructuring. Spread 'unpacks' an object. Rest 'packs' remaining properties into a new object.
